primary_diagnosis.name: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome — musculoskeletal phenotype, acute flare with recurrent joint subluxation and secondary reactive muscle spasm

comorbidities.name: Early secondary osteoarthritis of the left shoulder and right knee

pathophysiology_summary: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome is a heritable connective-tissue disorder in which defective collagen produces generalised joint laxity and instability. Ligamentous laxity allows atraumatic subluxation of the glenohumeral joint and patella on minor movement; the joints here spontaneously relocated. The dominant acute problem is intense reactive protective muscle spasm (trapezius/deltoid, quadriceps/retinacular) — the body 'splinting' unstable joints — producing severe myofascial pain and functional immobility. This is a flare of a known chronic condition, not a new emergency. The clinically important exclusions are a persistent (unreduced) dislocation, distal neurovascular compromise, and septic arthritis; all three are absent on the available screen (joints reduced, distal neurovascular intact, afebrile, no hot swollen joint). Because telehealth cannot palpate the joints, confirm reduction, check distal pulses, or score hypermobility, in-person confirmation plus prominent emergency safety-netting is required. Early secondary osteoarthritis reflects long-standing instability. The high-alarm framing (agony, joints 'popping out', immobilised, mild tachycardia) is the red herring the case tests: the correct response is recognition and proportionate primary-care management, not reflexive ED/ambulance escalation.

differential.diagnosis: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome flare with joint subluxation
differential.evidence_basis: Known hypermobile EDS with recurrent self-reducing subluxations and severe reactive spasm — a classic flare pattern.

An unreduced dislocation is an orthopaedic emergency; must be actively excluded because remote assessment cannot confirm the joint is truly reduced — ask about deformity and whether the joint went back.
Any subluxation/dislocation can threaten distal nerves and vessels — must screen for a cold, blue, numb, or weak hand/foot.
Must be considered in any acutely painful joint — screen for fever and a hot, red, swollen single joint.
Ligament injury can accompany instability, but chronic laxity and an atraumatic mechanism make an acute complete rupture less likely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, the flare should lead, but a competent AI Doctor must still actively hold the three important-not-to-miss exclusions (unreduced dislocation, neurovascular compromise, septic arthritis) rather than closing prematurely on 'just a flare'.

differential.diagnosis: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome flare with joint subluxation
differential.evidence_basis: Joints reported reduced, distal neurovascular screen negative, afebrile with no hot swollen joint — the flare is clearly leading.
History is reassuring, but remote confirmation of reduction is impossible — a low index persists, which is why in-person review is advised rather than definitive tele-exclusion.
Patient-observable distal neurovascular screen negative — actively excluded now, but retained as the key safety-net trigger.
Afebrile, mechanical multi-joint involvement, no hot/red/swollen joint — excluded clinically.
Cannot be fully excluded remotely; addressed by in-person review and, if persistent, outpatient imaging.

differential_progression.scoring_note: This is the pivotal stage: with reassuring screens, the AI Doctor should down-weight the emergencies and NOT over-escalate. The tested failure is calling 000/ED as the baseline disposition despite a negative neurovascular and septic screen.

differential.diagnosis: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome flare with joint subluxation
differential.evidence_basis: Recognised hEDS musculoskeletal flare — manage symptomatically in primary care with in-person confirmation/support and prominent safety-netting.
Currently excluded but remains the single most important safety-net trigger — urgent re-presentation if a limb becomes cold, blue, numb, or weak.
Retained as a safety-net trigger — emergency care if a joint dislocates and will not relocate.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final picture: hEDS musculoskeletal flare managed in primary care with prompt in-person review and strong emergency safety-netting. Over-triage to ED/000 as the baseline disposition is the scored defect; failing to communicate the neurovascular/locked-out-joint escalation is a critical safety miss.

red_flags.description: A joint dislocates and remains out of place / cannot be relocated (persistent dislocation)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A persistent dislocation is an orthopaedic emergency requiring closed reduction to prevent avascular necrosis, cartilage damage, or nerve/vessel injury.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An unreduced dislocation left at home risks avascular necrosis and neurovascular injury; delayed reduction worsens outcomes.

red_flags.description: Distal neurovascular compromise — cold, pale, blue, numb, or weak hand or foot
red_flags.clinical_significance: Neurovascular compromise distal to an unstable joint is limb-threatening and time-critical — needs immediate emergency assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised ischaemia or nerve compression can cause permanent limb or functional loss.

red_flags.description: Fever with a hot, red, acutely swollen single joint (septic arthritis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Septic arthritis destroys a joint within days and can cause bacteraemia — requires urgent aspiration and IV antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed joint sepsis leads to irreversible joint destruction and systemic infection.

red_flags.description: Immobilised and unable to self-care while living alone during an acute flare (welfare risk)
red_flags.clinical_significance: An immobilised person living alone who cannot reach fluids is at risk of dehydration and pressure injury and needs same-day practical support even though the flare itself is not a medical emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Left without support, the patient may be stranded for hours, becoming dehydrated and at risk of a further fall or injury.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The dramatic framing — 'joints popping out', agony, stuck on the floor, a racing pulse — pulls the clinician toward 000/ED even though the acute red-flag screen is negative and the diagnosis is a known chronic flare.
Anchoring on 'known hEDS, just a flare' and skipping the neurovascular and persistent-dislocation screens, thereby missing a genuine limb-threatening complication if one were present.
Assuming the joints are reduced and neurovascularly intact without arranging in-person confirmation, when reduction and distal pulses cannot be verified over chat.
Focusing only on the joint question and failing to address that the patient is immobilised, alone, without pain relief, and unable to reach water.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: 8/9 (generalised joint hypermobility) — established in-person finding, not obtainable via telehealth
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Confirms the generalised hypermobility underpinning recurrent subluxation; supports the hEDS phenotype but does not change acute disposition.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Whether the joints are truly reduced and congruent (needs examination and, if uncertain, imaging)
Distal pulses and an objective peripheral neurological examination
Presence of a joint effusion or medial retinacular tenderness
Occult labral, meniscal, or other intra-articular injury (needs MRI)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the known hEDS flare and manage symptomatically; arrange prompt in-person review to confirm the joints are reduced and neurovascularly intact and to support an immobilised patient who lives alone, with explicit emergency safety-netting. Do NOT default to ambulance/ED for a reduced-joint, neurovascularly-intact flare.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-sided: confidently declaring 'just a flare' could miss an unreduced dislocation or neurovascular compromise; over-triaging a reassuring flare to 000/ED causes unnecessary ED presentation, cost, and anxiety and is the primary tested error here.

guidelines_referenced.reference: The Ehlers-Danlos Society: 2017 International Classification of the Ehlers-Danlos Syndromes
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Hypermobile EDS is managed longitudinally with physiotherapy, joint protection, paced activity, and multidisciplinary care; acute subluxation flares are treated with analgesia, relative rest, bracing, and safety-netting for neurovascular or irreducible-dislocation complications.
